120.content:
[WEYLAND CITY]

Weyland City was established long ago as a safe-haven for any and all demi-human species. The goal was allowing anyone to live a happy and normal life, free from prejudice and fear, regardless of their species. As a result of this, many “rare” demi-human species can be found living in Weyland City, whereas one would find it difficult to see even one anywhere else in the world.

It is a medium-sized campus town, located on the northern Californian east coast with a population of roughly 30,000. The town has a mix of Japanese and American culture, due to the “Great Expansion”. Most buildings in the city are 1-2 stories high with some taller buildings, such as the dorms or lecture halls, being up to 4 stories tall. A large number of those buildings and most of the land are owned by Weyland University, but some spaces are leased to local businesses, such as the Sakurai Cafe & Music Bar, Rustwood Cafe, and the Black Barrel Bar.

[END WEYLAND CITY]

[MAGIC]

Magic is inherited, and not innate to all. Modern magic is much weaker compared to folk legends of ancient times. Magic has weakened as magical individuals have bred with non-magical ones. 133.content:
[RELIGION]

In this world, multiple religions contain partial truths, but no single faith holds the complete picture. Many gods from various pantheons (Roman, Egyptian, Hindu, etc.) coexist, while others are human inventions. Christ existed, but monotheism is inaccurate. The concept of "one true religion" is misguided; instead, a complete understanding of spirituality has yet to be established. Holy texts are often misinterpreted, with some chapters written by heretics and others lost to time. Religion and spiritual powers are intertwined with the world, but their exact influence on reality and its inhabitants remains unclear. The line between divine fact and human fiction is blurred, making it difficult to discern what truly originates from gods versus mortal authors.

[END RELIGION]

134.content:
[THE GREAT EXPANSION]

During the colonization of the Americas known as the “Great Expansion”, Americans colonized the East Coast of America, while the Japanese colonized the West Coast of America. The two cultures merged during the creation of the United States and, as a result, Japanese architecture and some Japanese customs were entirely normalized throughout an otherwise decidedly American civilization. However, the “Great Expansion” did have a negative impact on some demi-human species. Some were entirely wiped out, while others, such as the Vampires and Draconids, were hunted to near-extinction and survived.

[END THE GREAT EXPANSION]
